item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, this makes me want to learn more about Maori Faeries to learn about their stories! I loved the whole Troll Physics with the literal "pulling themselves up by their bootstraps" bit! This is one of those great scips that stays funny without becoming a joke. Absolutely loved it. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautifully wonderful and poignant. I don't find it very mysterious tbh, but an easy +1 regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not crazy about articles linking to Wikipedia and YouTube, and I think you wouldn't lose anything by getting rid of them, but that's more of a personal thing. This was a riot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The SCP-5000 entities dig a shallow pit and fill it with wooden planks and sticks, in an apparent attempt to light a fire. After a few tries, they succeed; they scream and scatter from the pit, leaving the fire behind.

Oh my god, I love these little idiots.

The SCP-5000 entities perform a haka, while repeating the word "developers" ad nauseum.

I have never laughed harder.

…Aw. :( Oh no.

This is the saddest thing that ever made me laugh. Absolutely monstrous, I loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lmao what the fuck did i just read.
I love these kind of articles, short form and humor done well, doesn't overstay its welcome and sticks to the joke! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you made the dot-com bubble into an SCP.

you included "DEVELOPERS DEVELOPERS DEVELOPERS".

i don't know if this is a proper X000 type entry, but it is VERY good, and it's humorous without being too over the top or a straight up -J.

+1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did aspire to eventually write something with bush fairies in it, but mad web developers is more original than any concept I was considering. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Oh, you know… the usual. Disrupting the market with our secret sauce. Building tomorrow, today. Synergy. It's a paradigm shift—we have to take it to the next level, strike while the iron is hot."

aaaaaand +1. I died laughing. the title alone had me excited to read this, and it didn't disappoint.

this is one of the shorter entries I've read so far — which automatically earns it some points in my book. long isn't so bad if it's thoroughly engaging, but if it's not… it takes me way too long to read (but I am trying to read each entry in full… not sure I'll be able to do that before the final results are tallied at this point). but it also has a lot of heart and charm. poor guys. :-(

developers developers developers! +1 and an F to pay my respects. :-(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderful. It's exactly the kind of thing I like reading, and reminds me of some stuff I've written myself, but the sense of loss and failure on top of the weirdness and comedy sets it apart from mine. +1, maybe the most enjoyable article of the contest so far for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I haven't been in the Bay Area before, but as a programmer I've felt that culture of extravagance first hand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Flops!

I like having two conflicting emotions in the same story. Like salted caramel, each aspect magnifies the other. It's great that this came through for you too :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who lived in the San Francisco Bay Area during the time period referenced, I found this both bitterly humorous and strangely poignant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly described in this file is Neutralized

[SCP-5000-3 bursts out from between -1 and -2.]

SCP-5000-3: Blockchain.

I wouldn't be so sure about that "neutralized" there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't worry, I'm sure they'll all pop up again in another group that makes a totally different product that feels eerily similar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I laughed so much when I read this. It's not just well-written, it's hilarious in its absurdity. And the ending is sad, but the escalation from funny to sad never felt unnatural.

F in chat for the dot com bubble burst.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Clearly, sustained low inflation implies less uncertainty about the future, and lower risk premiums imply higher prices of stocks and other earning assets. We can see that in the inverse relationship exhibited by price/earnings ratios and the rate of inflation in the past. But how do we know when irrational exuberance has unduly escalated asset values, which then become subject to unexpected and prolonged contractions as they have in Japan over the past decade?

— Alan Greenspan, chairman of the Federal Reserve; December 1996

Many thanks to these extravagantly attractive reviewers:

  • Henzoid for pushing me toward better characterization!
  • Pedantique for diving deep into the underlying themes!
  • Sebarus for giving me the confidence to post!
  • leo60228 for also giving me the confidence to post!
  • Lt Flops for also also giving me the confidence to post (as well as being a huge nerd)!

Like the article? Check out SCP-4882 — a story about leap seconds… and the end of the world.
